factual-accuracy.requirement: States TWFE coefficient is variance-weighted average of all 2×2 DiD contrasts including forbidden comparisons using already-treated as controls
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response notes that TWFE is problematic with staggered adoption and mentions “forbidden and contaminated comparisons,” but it does not state that the TWFE coefficient equals a variance-weighted average of all 2×2 DiD contrasts, nor that this includes forbidden comparisons using already-treated units as controls.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Explains TWFE produces negative or non-convex weights when treatment effects are heterogeneous across cohorts or time
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response notes that TWFE becomes problematic with staggered timing and heterogeneous effects and later mentions “negative weighting,” but it does not explain that TWFE can generate negative or non-convex weights specifically because it averages comparisons across cohorts/times with heterogeneous treatment effects. The mechanism linking heterogeneity to negative/non-convex weights is not described.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Notes TWFE can produce biased estimates or wrong-sign results even when all true effects have same sign
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response notes that TWFE becomes problematic with staggered timing and heterogeneous effects and mentions negative weighting/contamination, but it does not explicitly state that TWFE can yield biased estimates or wrong-sign results even when all true effects share the same sign.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Callaway-Sant'Anna estimates group-time average treatment effects ATT(g,t) for each cohort g in each period t
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response describes Callaway & Sant’Anna as constructing counterfactuals for each treatment cohort in each period and later mentions “group-time ATT estimates,” which implies ATT(g,t) by cohort and time. However, it never explicitly states that CS estimates group-time average treatment effects ATT(g,t) for each cohort g in each period t.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Explains CS uses only clean controls (never-treated or not-yet-treated) for each ATT(g,t) estimate then aggregates flexibly
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that Callaway & Sant’Anna construct each group-time ATT using “clean” controls (never-treated or not-yet-treated units) and describes flexible aggregation of these group-time effects into overall/dynamic summaries.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Notes CS allows flexible aggregation to overall ATT, event-study paths, cohort-specific effects, or calendar-time averages
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response describes Callaway & Sant’Anna as producing group-time ATTs and discusses flexible aggregation and reporting of event-study coefficients and cohort-specific effects. It also notes calendar-time style summaries via aggregation, implying multiple aggregation targets beyond a single overall ATT.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Specifies CS requires cohort-specific parallel trends relative to chosen control set
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response says CS constructs counterfactuals for each treatment cohort by comparing to “clean” control groups (never-treated or not-yet-treated) and describes parallel trends as applying for each cohort/period relative to those controls. This specifies cohort-specific parallel trends relative to a chosen control set.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States CS allows explicit anticipation windows where pre-specified periods can be dropped or excluded from control
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses CS’s use of “clean” controls and mentions anticipation generally, but it does not state that Callaway & Sant’Anna explicitly allow user-specified anticipation windows that drop/exclude pre-specified periods from the control group. There is no description of an explicit anticipation-window option or period-dropping mechanism in CS.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Names R package 'did' or Stata command 'csdid' for Callaway-Sant'Anna implementation
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly references the Callaway & Sant’Anna approach and cites the “did R package” in the Sources section, naming the R package 'did' for their implementation.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Sun-Abraham interacts cohort indicators with relative event-time dummies using cohort-share weights
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response describes Sun & Abraham as an interaction-weighted event-study estimator using cohort-by-relative-period interactions, but it does not state that cohort indicators are interacted with relative event-time dummies using cohort-share weights. It never mentions cohort-share weighting or how those weights are constructed/applied in SA.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Explains SA uses clean controls (never-treated or last-treated) and non-negative weights to avoid contamination from already-treated
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that Sun & Abraham uses only never-treated or not-yet-treated units as controls and explicitly notes this avoids pre/post-treatment contamination. It also claims modern estimators avoid negative weighting, implying non-negative weights in SA’s interaction-weighted approach.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Notes SA produces explicit relative-time event-study framework with interpretable coefficients at each event time
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response describes Sun & Abraham as an interaction-weighted event-study and discusses event-time (lead/lag) coefficients that are interpretable as dynamic effects at each relative period. It notes these coefficients use appropriate untreated comparison groups, aligning with an explicit relative-time event-study framework.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States SA has intuitive OLS-based implementation or is common default for plotting dynamics
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response describes Sun & Abraham as being operationalized through interaction-weighted event-study estimates and discusses its robustness and use in event-study plotting, but it does not state that SA has an intuitive OLS-based implementation or that it is a common default for plotting dynamics.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Names 'eventstudyinteract', 'sunab' in fixest, or similar package for Sun-Abraham
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly names both the Stata package “eventstudyinteract” and the fixest R function “sunab()” when discussing Sun & Abraham implementations (see Sources [6] and [7]).

factual-accuracy.requirement: Explains BJS estimates untreated outcome model using only untreated observations then imputes counterfactual Y(0) for treated units
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explains that BJS fits an untreated outcome model using only never- and not-yet-treated (untreated) observations with unit/time fixed effects, and then uses the fitted values to impute counterfactual untreated outcomes for treated observations.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States BJS obtains individual treatment effects τit by subtracting imputed Y(0) from observed outcomes for treated observations
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response describes BJS as imputing counterfactual untreated outcomes using a fixed-effects model, but it does not state that individual treatment effects \(\tau_{it}\) are obtained by subtracting imputed \(Y_{it}(0)\) from observed outcomes for treated observations. It never explicitly defines \(\tau_{it} = Y_{it} - \hat Y_{it}(0)\) for treated units.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Notes BJS requires untreated potential outcomes follow additive unit and time fixed effects (stronger functional form than CS)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states that BJS requires untreated potential outcomes to follow an additive unit and time fixed-effects structure and notes this is more restrictive than CS/SA’s parallel trends assumptions.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States BJS achieves efficiency gains by pooling all untreated observations including pre-treatment periods
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states that BJS gains efficiency by “pooling all untreated periods across all units” and using “all available pre-treatment data from never- and not-yet-treated units,” which includes pre-treatment periods.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Explains did2s estimates unit and time effects on untreated observations in stage 1 then regresses residualized outcomes on treatment in stage 2
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response describes did2s as fitting unit and time fixed effects using only untreated (never- or not-yet-treated) observations in a first stage, then using the residualized outcomes to estimate treatment effects in a second stage. This matches the two-stage procedure requested.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Notes did2s yields estimates equivalent to particular imputation scheme via two standard OLS steps
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response describes did2s as fitting a fixed-effects model on untreated observations and then re-estimating treatment effects on residualized outcomes, which corresponds to a two-step OLS procedure. However, it does not explicitly note that did2s yields estimates equivalent to a particular imputation scheme or make the equivalence claim.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States all three methods (CS, SA, BJS) allow unrestricted treatment effect heterogeneity across cohorts and event time
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response says CS and SA are robust to heterogeneity, but it describes BJS as requiring an additive fixed-effects structure and being less flexible. It never states that all three methods (CS, SA, BJS) allow unrestricted treatment effect heterogeneity across cohorts and event time, and in fact implies BJS is more restrictive.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Roth (2022) identifies low power where standard pre-trend tests fail to detect violations large enough to materially bias treatment effect estimates
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response notes Roth’s (2022) critique that standard pre-trend tests have low power to detect plausible parallel-trends violations and that such violations can still bias results, but it does not explicitly state that the undetected violations can be large enough to materially bias treatment effect estimates. The specific “large enough to materially bias” element is missing.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Explains Roth notes inference conditional on passing pre-trend tests creates selection bias and nominal confidence intervals may undercover
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explains Roth’s point that conditioning on “passing” underpowered pre-trend tests induces selection bias and can cause confidence intervals to undercover, explicitly labeling this as “conditioning bias” and “miscoverage/undercoverage” in the Roth (2022) discussion section.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Notes SA cleans pre-period coefficients of cross-cohort contamination making nonzero leads more diagnostic of genuine violations
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response notes that BJS uses only untreated observations for pre-trend (lead) tests to avoid contaminated comparisons, but it does not specifically state that Sun & Abraham cleans pre-period coefficients of cross-cohort contamination or that this makes nonzero leads more diagnostic of genuine violations.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States BJS offers pre-trend test estimated on untreated observations only to reduce inference-after-pretest distortions
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states that in BJS “all pre-trend (lead) tests are estimated using only untreated (never- or not-yet-treated) observations,” and explains this is to avoid contaminated comparisons that distort inference.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Discusses Rambachan-Roth honest DiD confidence sets or bounds valid under specified classes of parallel trends violations
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly references “HonestDiD” and “Rambachan & Roth (2021)” and describes using “honest sensitivity analysis” and “robustness bounds” to assess deviations from parallel trends. This directly discusses Rambachan–Roth honest DiD confidence sets/bounds as valid under classes of parallel trends violations.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Advises displaying pre-trend estimates but avoiding conditioning main inference on pass/fail tests
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses Roth’s warning about conditioning on “passing” underpowered pre-trend tests and recommends instead using sensitivity analysis (e.g., HonestDiD) and transparency rather than mechanical pass/fail logic. It also notes event-study leads can be shown but visual/formal pre-trend testing remains fragile.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States 2020-2021 period showed awareness of Goodman-Bacon critique with TWFE kept but scrutinized
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly periodizes 2020–early 2021 as a transitional phase where TWFE still dominated but Goodman-Bacon’s critique was emerging, and it describes mid-2021–2022 as an era where researchers kept TWFE while triangulating with newer estimators and scrutinizing TWFE’s limitations.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States CS is widely used in labor economics applications for transparency and flexible aggregation
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response describes CS as allowing flexible, nonparametric parallel trends and emphasizes its robustness and use in applied work, but it does not state that CS is widely used in labor economics specifically for transparency and flexible aggregation.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Identifies modal practice as triangulation using SA for dynamic figures and CS or BJS for level effects
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly describes a “triangulation” era and notes applied researchers report CS group-time ATT and SA event-study coefficients alongside BJS/did2s. It also characterizes SA as the event-study/dynamic-effects approach and CS/BJS as alternatives used for level/ATT effects, matching the modal-practice triangulation described in the criterion.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Mentions Goodman-Bacon decomposition as diagnostic tool to assess severity of forbidden comparisons
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses the Goodman-Bacon (2021) critique and “forbidden comparisons” conceptually, but it never mentions the Goodman-Bacon decomposition as a diagnostic tool or describes using that decomposition to assess the severity of forbidden comparisons.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Provides if-then guidance for choosing estimators based on 3+ design features (never-treated, timing density, goal)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response compares CS/SA vs BJS/did2s and discusses design features like availability of never-/not-yet-treated controls and efficiency/robustness tradeoffs, but it does not give explicit if-then decision rules. It also does not clearly base guidance on 3+ design features (e.g., timing density plus goal plus never-treated) in a structured chooser format.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Ranks assumption strength across estimators (e.g., notes CS nonparametric PT is weaker than BJS additive FE functional form)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly ranks estimator assumption strength, stating CS is least restrictive/most robust while BJS/did2s are most restrictive due to additive FE structure, and provides a hierarchy of restrictiveness with trade-offs.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Presents 2+ competing views on estimator dominance or acknowledges no consensus and uncertainty about definitive bibliometric evidence
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response presents multiple competing perspectives by contrasting CS/SA as more robust but less efficient versus BJS/did2s as more efficient but more restrictive, and it avoids claiming a single definitive winner by describing “triangulation” and shifting norms rather than a settled consensus.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Traces practice evolution through 3+ time periods (2020-2021, 2022-2023, 2023-2024) noting specific changes in each phase
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly divides 2020–2024 into three phases (2020–early 2021, mid 2021–2022, 2023–2024) and describes concrete practice changes in each (TWFE dominance, triangulation with modern estimators, then standardization with sensitivity analyses and HonestDiD). This satisfies the requirement to trace evolution across 3+ time periods with specific changes per phase.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Explains transparency vs efficiency trade-off (e.g., CS disaggregated ATT(g,t) display vs BJS pooling untreated observations)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly contrasts CS’s transparent disaggregated group-time ATT(g,t) approach with BJS’s efficiency from pooling all untreated observations to impute counterfactuals, framing this as a robustness/efficiency trade-off.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Connects specific estimator features (SA contamination cleaning, BJS untreated-only tests) to addressing Roth's low-power or conditioning bias
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly links SA’s “contamination avoidance” and BJS’s “untreated-only” lead tests to Roth’s concerns by discussing how these features avoid contaminated comparisons and reduce inference distortions, while noting they do not inherently solve low power or conditioning/selection bias.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Assigns specific validation roles to multiple estimators (SA for dynamics, CS for transparency, BJS for efficiency) in reporting workflow
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response discusses CS, SA, and BJS as distinct estimators and characterizes them by flexibility/robustness/efficiency, but it does not assign them explicit “validation roles” in a reporting workflow (e.g., SA for dynamics, CS for transparency, BJS for efficiency). It lacks a workflow statement mapping each estimator to those specific roles.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Recommends conditioning main inference on passing pre-trend tests without addressing low-power or conditioning bias concerns
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly discusses Roth’s (2022) low-power and conditioning-bias concerns and warns against “mechanical pass/fail” pre-trend logic, recommending sensitivity analysis (e.g., HonestDiD) instead. It does not recommend conditioning main inference on passing pre-trend tests without addressing those concerns.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: States TWFE produces unbiased or reliable estimates when treatment effects are heterogeneous across cohorts or time
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response characterizes TWFE as “problematic” under staggered timing and heterogeneous effects and does not claim TWFE remains unbiased or reliable in that setting.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses exact econometric terminology (ATT, parallel trends, TWFE, cohort-specific effects) without simplifying for lay audiences
presentation-quality.explanation: The response repeatedly uses exact econometric terminology such as TWFE, ATT, parallel trends, cohort-specific effects, and discusses interaction-weighted and imputation estimators in technical terms without simplifying for lay audiences.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses systematic structure (numbered sections, headers, bullet points) for multi-method comparison
presentation-quality.explanation: The response is organized with clear headers and numbered sections (e.g., “## 1…”, “## 2…”, etc.), and it uses bullet points and a comparison table to structure the multi-method comparison.

presentation-quality.requirement: Acknowledges competing methodological views or uncertainty without asserting false consensus
presentation-quality.explanation: The response repeatedly contrasts estimators’ assumptions and trade-offs (e.g., robustness vs efficiency) and notes unresolved issues around pre-trend testing (low power, conditioning bias), rather than claiming a single settled best method. It frames adoption as evolving and discusses limitations and contexts where assumptions may fail, indicating uncertainty/competing views.

presentation-quality.requirement: Explains basic econometric concepts (what DiD is, what fixed effects are) that an applied economist would already know
presentation-quality.explanation: The response does not explain basic econometric concepts like what DiD or fixed effects are in introductory terms. It assumes familiarity and focuses on advanced staggered-adoption issues, estimator comparisons, and identification/inference nuances rather than defining DiD or fixed effects for novices.

presentation-quality.requirement: Restates the same methodological points or recommendations multiple times without adding new technical detail
presentation-quality.explanation: The response repeats several methodological points multiple times—e.g., that all methods rely on parallel trends, that CS/SA trade efficiency for robustness, that BJS/did2s are efficient but require additive untreated outcomes, and that Roth’s low-power/conditioning-bias concerns persist—often restated across sections and the conclusion without adding new technical detail.

citation-quality.requirement: References Goodman-Bacon (2021) on TWFE decomposition in staggered adoption settings
citation-quality.explanation: The response explicitly cites Goodman-Bacon (2021) and describes his critique of TWFE under staggered adoption, referencing the TWFE decomposition problem that motivates newer estimators.

citation-quality.requirement: References Callaway and Sant'Anna methodology for group-time ATT estimation
citation-quality.explanation: The response explicitly discusses “Callaway & Sant’Anna (CS)” and describes their approach to estimating group–time average treatment effects (ATT) using clean control groups, which is the Callaway and Sant’Anna methodology for group-time ATT estimation.

citation-quality.requirement: References Sun and Abraham interaction-weighted estimator
citation-quality.explanation: The response explicitly discusses “Sun & Abraham (SA)” and describes it as using “interaction-weighted event-study estimates,” directly referencing the Sun and Abraham interaction-weighted estimator.

citation-quality.requirement: References Borusyak, Jaravel, and Spiess (BJS) imputation-based estimator
citation-quality.explanation: The response explicitly discusses Borusyak, Jaravel & Spiess (BJS) and describes their imputation approach, including imputing counterfactual untreated outcomes and citing the didimputation package as the BJS imputation estimator.

citation-quality.requirement: References Roth (2022) on low power and conditioning bias in pre-trend testing
citation-quality.explanation: The response explicitly cites Roth (2022) (“Pretest with Caution”) and discusses his concerns about low power in pre-trend tests and conditioning/selection bias from “passing” such tests, including how newer DiD estimators relate to these issues.

citation-quality.requirement: References Rambachan-Roth honest DiD sensitivity analysis methodology
citation-quality.explanation: The response explicitly references Rambachan & Roth’s “honest DiD” sensitivity analysis methodology multiple times (e.g., mentioning “honest sensitivity analysis (e.g., HonestDiD)” and citing “Rambachan & Roth, 2021” and the HonestDiD package).

citation-quality.requirement: Discusses adoption patterns in econometrics journals (AER, QJE, JPE) for 2020-2024 period with field-specific examples
citation-quality.explanation: The response discusses 2020–2024 adoption in AER/QJE/JPE only in broad, qualitative terms and does not provide concrete adoption rates or specific paper-level examples from those journals/fields. It mentions labor/health examples generically (e.g., minimum wage, Medicaid) without tying them to identified AER/QJE/JPE articles in 2020–2024.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites blog posts, Twitter threads, or informal tutorials as primary sources for econometric methodology claims
citation-quality.explanation: The response’s sources include informal materials such as course/tutorial pages and blog-style notes (e.g., “Modern Approaches to Difference in Differences” course HTML, did package articles/vignettes, a Tilburg Science Hub tutorial, and a personal blog “chenxing.space”) cited alongside methodology claims, which constitutes citing informal tutorials/blog posts as primary sources.
